unitTitle: GCSE Databases and SQL (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Database essentials — tables, records, fields and keys
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what a database is
I can name the parts of a database (table, record, field, primary key)
I can describe a flat file database
I can describe a relational database and why it is used
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: database · table · record · field · primary key · foreign key · redundancy. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a copy of dbMusic.db ready in DB Browser for SQLite. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — how is data stored? Pupils look at the DVLA vehicle data on the starter worksheet and CHOOSE how it is stored, then label the parts of a music table (drag Table/Field/Record onto A/B/C).
   • Support: matching — term to meaning.  • Core: recall what a primary key is.  • Challenge: multi-select the properties of a primary key.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — name the parts. Teacher points at a table: field = column, record = row, table = the grid, primary key = the unique field (TrackID). Likely error: “a table is a database” → fix-words “a table lives INSIDE a database.”
3. (20–30) Flat file vs relational. Show the inefficient flat file (Sara Bibi repeated). Name the problems: data redundancy and data inconsistency. Pupils SORT facts into flat-file vs relational on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: choose how many tables each has.  • Core: fill the blanks linking primary key to foreign key.  • Challenge: explain redundancy and inconsistency.
4. (30–50) You-do — explore the DBMS. Play the DB Browser demo video (teacher hook), then pupils open a COPY of dbMusic.db, find the three tables, and screenshot the structure onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil names a table/record/field on screen.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: SQL searches — SELECT, WHERE and ORDER BY
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what SQL is for
I can use SQL to retrieve data from a table
I can sort the results with ORDER BY
I can use SQL to retrieve data from more than one table
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: SQL · SELECT · FROM · WHERE · ORDER BY · operator. TA: dbMusic.db open in DB Browser for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — fill in the database words from last lesson on the starter worksheet (database, table, relational, foreign key).
   • Support: choose the right word.  • Core: define a foreign key.  • Challenge: why a relational database avoids repeated data.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do — what is SQL? SELECT (which columns), FROM (which table), WHERE (the condition). Read a query line by line, then live-code it; play the live-coding demo video as the hook. Pupils SORT operators into comparison vs logical.
3. (20–40) You-do — retrieve data. Pupils write SELECT queries and drag the query lines into order (build the query). Then ORDER BY to sort.
   • Support: match each comparison operator to its meaning.  • Core: complete and write a WHERE query.  • Challenge: a multi-table query. Likely error: missing speech marks / wrong spelling → fix-words “match the spelling in the table exactly.”
4. (40–52) Retrieve from more than one table — name columns in SELECT, list tables in FROM, link primary key to foreign key in WHERE (book-shop example image). Second live-coding demo as the hook.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows one query that worked.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Insert, update, delete — data types and changing data
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what a data type is and name common ones
I can choose a suitable data type for a field
I can write INSERT, UPDATE and DELETE queries
I can prove a query worked using SELECT
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: data type · INTEGER · TEXT · REAL · INSERT · UPDATE · DELETE. TA: dbMusic.db open for everyone. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — data types. Pupils SORT a doctor-surgery’s fields into INTEGER / TEXT / REAL on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: choose what an INTEGER/TEXT stores.  • Core: match each data type to what it stores.  • Challenge: why a date of birth is stored as TEXT in SQLite.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE. Worked example of each on the music database. Stress: text needs speech marks; ONE semicolon at the end; always WHERE. Likely error: semicolon on every line → fix-words “one semicolon, at the very end.”
3. (25–45) You-do — pupils write queries on their music database. First drag the INSERT lines into order, then write an UPDATE (Angry Pete → Happy Pete) and a DELETE.
   • Support: choose which query adds/removes/changes.  • Core: complete and write an UPDATE/INSERT.  • Challenge: a DELETE plus a SELECT to prove it.
4. (45–52) Prove it worked — after every change, run a SELECT. Build the habit: change the data, then check it.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows one query that worked. TA notes who proved it with a SELECT.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Swim challenge (part 1) — build and interrogate a database
lessons.objectives:
I can identify the primary and foreign keys in linked tables
I can complete a CREATE TABLE script with the right data types
I can write SELECT queries to interrogate a database
I can write UPDATE and DELETE queries to maintain a database
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: interrogate · CREATE · primary key · foreign key · one-to-many. TA: dbSwim.db ready in DB Browser; the swim challenge worksheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — recap keys and relationships. Pupils SORT the tblLessons fields into primary key / foreign key on the starter worksheet (LessonID is the PK; CourseID + MemberID are FKs).
   • Support: choose what a primary/foreign key is.  • Core: explain one-to-many.  • Challenge: why a foreign key can repeat but a primary key cannot.
2. (10–20) Set the scene — a leisure centre needs a database. Two tables already exist; pupils build tblLessons. Drag the plan steps into order (work out keys → CREATE with data types → INSERT → SELECT → UPDATE/DELETE).
3. (20–50) You-do — the swim challenge. Complete the CREATE script (key fields are INTEGER), INSERT the test data, then SELECT to interrogate. Drag the multi-table SELECT lines into order first.
   • Support: fill the data type + choose the read-only query.  • Core: write a SELECT for courses with > 6 sessions.  • Challenge: an UPDATE to move Ducklings into Dippers plus a SELECT to prove it. Likely error: forgetting WHERE → fix-words “no WHERE changes EVERY row.” TA: “prompt, do not type it for them.”
4. (50–55) Plenary — three quick recap questions on mini-whiteboards (unique field? what links tables? which query removes?). Reassure: more time next lesson.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Swim challenge (part 2) and end-of-unit assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can read an SQL query and predict what it changes
I can finish and test the swim database
I can answer exam-style questions on databases and SQL
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: predict · interrogate · update · delete. TA: dbSwim.db + the swim challenge worksheet open; the end-of-unit quiz worksheet ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — read the query. Pupils read SQL queries on the starter worksheet and predict what each does to the data.
   • Support: match each query word to what it does.  • Core: predict what an UPDATE changes.  • Challenge: explain exactly which records a DELETE…BETWEEN removes and why WHERE matters.
2. (5–30) Finish the swim database — pupils continue the swim challenge tasks 1–4 from last lesson, looking back at earlier lessons for help. Calm independent work. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (30–53) Assessment — the end-of-unit quiz worksheet (exam-style). Speed-camera flat-file scenario: choose the primary key + data types, describe a flat-file problem, read a query’s output, then write SELECT / INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE queries. Exam conditions. Fix-words on the board: “text needs speech marks; always WHERE; one semicolon at the end.”
4. (53–55) End of unit — recap the journey: database → relational database with keys → SQL SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation: the starter is on the SAME task at three depths; the quiz is a shared assessment everyone sits. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
